quick_facts.label: Official status of the role
quick_facts.value: “Permanent Airman” is best understood as a taxonomy label rather than an official rank title: after the four-year Agnipath engagement, selected Agniveers enter the Indian Air Force’s regular cadre as airmen. Government policy describes this as permanent enrolment/regular-cadre enrolment, not an officer commission.

quick_facts.label: Selection route
quick_facts.value: An Agniveer may apply after completing four years. Selection is centralised and based on objective criteria including performance during the four-year engagement, organisational requirements, and policies in force; up to 25% of a specific Agniveer batch may be enrolled in the regular cadre. It is an opportunity to apply, not an entitlement to retention.

quick_facts.label: Further service commitment
quick_facts.value: The Cabinet’s June 14, 2022 Agnipath announcement states that people selected into the regular cadre must serve for a further minimum engagement period of 15 years, under the then-existing and subsequently amended terms for the Indian Air Force equivalent of Other Ranks.

quick_facts.label: Airman rank ladder
quick_facts.value: The Indian Air Force lists the airman hierarchy, from junior to senior, as Aircraftman, Leading Aircraftman, Corporal, Sergeant, Junior Warrant Officer, Warrant Officer, and Master Warrant Officer.

sections.heading: From Agniveervayu to the regular airmen cadre
paragraphs: This node applies only after an individual has completed the four-year Agnipath engagement as an Agniveer in the Indian Air Force. At that point, the person may be offered the opportunity to apply for permanent enrolment, subject to the organisation’s requirements and policies then in force.

The process is centralised and uses objective criteria that include performance over the four-year engagement. The policy provides for enrolment of up to 25% of each specific Agniveer batch into the regular cadre, so completion of the initial term alone does not guarantee selection. For selected personnel, the stated further engagement is a minimum of 15 years and service is governed by the applicable IAF Other Ranks-equivalent terms as amended over time.

The phrase “permanent commission” should be used cautiously for this node. Official materials distinguish an officer’s commission from enrolment in the regular airmen cadre; the IAF identifies this personnel group as “Airmen.”
bullets: Prerequisite: completion of the four-year Agniveer engagement.
Selection basis: organisational requirement, applicable policy, and centralised objective assessment including performance.
Selection ceiling: up to 25% of a particular Agniveer batch.
Outcome: regular-cadre enrolment as an airman, with a stated further minimum engagement of 15 years.

sections.heading: What a regular IAF airman does
paragraphs: Duties depend on the allotted trade and unit rather than on a single uniform job description. The IAF describes airmen as providing support essential to its mission, including technical maintenance support for IAF inventory, runway-related work, communications, human-resources management, security, firefighting, and driving special vehicles. A permanent airman should therefore expect a disciplined, service-directed role in which daily work, training, and location are determined by operational requirements.
bullets: Technical support and maintenance of IAF inventory.
Operational-support work involving airfields/runways and communications.
Administrative, security, firefighting, or special-vehicle duties where assigned.
Continuous adherence to service discipline and unit procedures.

sections.heading: Capabilities developed and used in the role
paragraphs: The Agnipath policy states that Agniveers receive military skills and experience along with discipline, physical fitness, leadership, courage, and related service attributes. In the regular cadre, the IAF’s airmen role also calls for trade-specific capability in areas such as maintenance, communications, security, firefighting, vehicle operations, or personnel support. The precise technical skills required will therefore depend on the airman’s assigned stream and posting.
bullets: Military discipline, physical fitness, teamwork, and leadership.
Technical aptitude for the assigned maintenance, communications, or operational-support function.
Safety-conscious working practices, especially in airfield, equipment, security, and firefighting environments.
Adaptability to unit-assigned duties and service requirements.

sections.heading: Work setting
paragraphs: The workplace is an Indian Air Force unit or establishment assigned according to service needs. The official airmen description points to operational and support environments that may include maintenance settings, airfields and runway operations, communication functions, personnel administration, security, firefighting, and special-vehicle operations. The exact station, trade, shift pattern, and exposure to field or hardship conditions are variable rather than fixed features of the role.
bullets: IAF stations and units, with duties allocated by trade and operational requirement.
Technical, airfield, communications, administrative, security, or emergency-response settings.
Potentially varied conditions because risk and hardship allowances are described as applicable according to service circumstances.

sections.heading: Rank progression after regular enrolment
paragraphs: The IAF’s published airmen rank structure begins with Aircraftman and then proceeds to Leading Aircraftman, Corporal, Sergeant, Junior Warrant Officer, Warrant Officer, and Master Warrant Officer. The IAF expressly says that an airman joins as an Aircraftman and subsequently climbs the ladder. This establishes Leading Aircraftman as the next listed rank above Aircraftman, but it does not by itself establish a guaranteed timeline or a universal examination rule for promotion; applicable promotion policies, vacancies, trade requirements, courses, and assessments should be checked through current service orders.
bullets: Entry point in the published airman ladder: Aircraftman.
Next listed rank: Leading Aircraftman.
Higher listed ranks: Corporal, Sergeant, Junior Warrant Officer, Warrant Officer, and Master Warrant Officer.
Promotion timing and requirements should not be assumed from the rank chart alone.

sections.heading: Compensation and benefits context
paragraphs: Compensation for a regular-cadre airman should not be conflated with the four-year Agniveer package. The June 14, 2022 Cabinet announcement set out the Agniveer package, the associated corpus contribution, and Seva Nidhi exit arrangement specifically for the initial four-year engagement; it also stated that Agniveers during that engagement had no entitlement to gratuity or pensionary benefits. By contrast, personnel selected for regular enrolment move to the applicable IAF Other Ranks-equivalent service terms, which the policy says may be amended from time to time.

Accordingly, any current pay figure, allowance, pension treatment, or benefit entitlement for this node is variable by rank, posting, duty conditions, and prevailing government/IAF rules. It should be verified against the current pay matrix and service orders at the time of enrolment or promotion rather than inferred from Agniveer compensation tables.
bullets: The Agniveer monthly package and Seva Nidhi are four-year-engagement provisions, not a reliable salary quote for a regular airman.
Regular-cadre terms are governed by the applicable IAF Other Ranks-equivalent rules and may change.
Allowances can vary with duty and hardship/risk conditions.
